critic_raw:
BASELINE ASSESSMENT:

The video platform's claim that a 6% session watch-time increase demonstrates the new feed model surfaces more relevant content is not supported because the measurement window contains multiple independent confounders.

In week two of the four-week rollout, the platform launched a paid promotion campaign for creator channels tied to a major sports event. These creators also received preferential placement in push notifications and in-app banners that are independent of the feed ranker. High-quality event content with promoted discovery mechanisms is a direct, independent mechanism for increasing session watch time. The platform does not separate sessions initiated from the home feed from sessions reactivated by push notifications, so the aggregate watch-time metric conflates the ranker's contribution with the notification and promotion channels' contributions. The old and new rankers were not both replayed on the same request logs, so traffic composition changes are confounded with model quality changes.

Stable ad load is one positive control but does not address the promotion campaign and entrypoint mixing. The new model's design improvements (larger candidate pool, new loss function) are real but do not establish the causal attribution.

The new ranker may genuinely improve engagement on ordinary home-feed requests. That claim requires: (1) separating home-feed-initiated sessions from notification-driven sessions, (2) excluding event-promotion traffic, and (3) log-replaying both models on the same requests.

Verdict: empirical_test_agreed.
